author: @dgpreston5593
text: Well done, Luke. I enjoy using PWM with arduino projects. And you may also enjoy an h-bridge hat with your arduino. With it, you can show motor speed control in both forward and backward directions, still using PWM.  But be careful driving an LED "in reverse"! They don't fair nearly as well.  Keep the videos coming, great fun.

author: @Wilksey37
text: PWM essentially works by turning on and off many times (creates a square wave), the frequency is fixed to around 490Hz on the Uno at 16MHz, the time on to time off is called the duty cycle, so 50% duty would be off for half the time and on for half the time, the way our eyes perceive light at such a fast rate (which is how some LED matrix displays work) is called persistence of vision so turning a output in on and off fast the LED reacts to this by appearing "dim" but in reality the voltage is being given and taken away so quickly that it hasn't time to turn fully off, and we can't see these transitions we just see a "dim" LED.

author: @mamodmahmod708
text: I ask you to help me ... as I make battery of aluminum foil ... and please which liquid should be ... ???

replies.author: @wanderingzanzey2126
replies.text: Aluminium chloride hexahydrate with urea and choline chloride in a glycerol matrix. Then Al as the negative, a porous and dielectric separator material, and carbon or Ni foam as the positive electrode. Is rechargable, average voltage of 1.8 - 2.5V per cell and over 600mA/g

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: best not posting this kind of question to Luke mate - he doesn't know the answer -  Zacariah gave a good answer

replies.author: @wanderingzanzey2126
replies.text: I actually made a mistake here, i am going to mention it just for other people readign the comments - Glycerol is incorrect. I meant to say Ethylene Glycol. Not Glycerol. The electrolyte is a Deep Eutectic Solvent with AlCl as the active ion.

author: @sidneytwining1958
text: May this arrangement handle large currents/power?  What would be different?

replies.author: @Wilksey37
replies.text: No, the Arduino can only handle 20-30mA of current (check spec) you would need a FET or something to drive large currents.
